businessModel.text:
Costco operates on a high-volume, low-margin membership warehouse model that creates a near-unbreakable competitive moat:
  
    Membership Fees: The core economic engine. Costco charges an annual membership fee (Gold Star $65, Business $65, Executive $130) that is nearly pure profit. With ~76 million paid members and a renewal rate exceeding 93 per cent, this is one of the most predictable revenue streams in retail.
    Warehouse Sales: A curated selection of approximately 4,000 SKUs across groceries, electronics, appliances, apparel, tyres, and home goods. Costco sells at thin gross margins (approximately 11–12 per cent net of membership benefit) but extremely high inventory turnover.
    E-commerce and ancillary services: Costco.com for home delivery, Costco Travel, optical centres, hearing aid centres, pharmacies, and food courts — all serving to deepen member engagement and increase visit frequency.
    Kirkland Signature: The proprietary Kirkland Signature brand delivers higher-margin private-label products across food, apparel, and household goods — increasingly important to margin stability.
  
  Costco's genius is using ultra-low prices to attract members, then generating nearly all its operating profit from membership fees while the merchandise sales cover costs. It is one of the most capital-efficient retail models ever constructed.

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull Case
      75 / 100
      Member count grows to 85–90 million with rising renewal rates. The Executive membership mix continues to improve, lifting effective per-member revenue. International expansion (particularly in Asia) adds material revenue and profit. The shares re-rate to 55×+ as the market prices in the durability of the franchise. Total annual returns of 12–15 per cent from a combination of comp growth and multiple expansion.
      Weight: 30%
    
    
      Base Case
      65 / 100
      Steady mid-single-digit comparable sales growth, membership fee increases every 5–7 years, and continued expansion internationally. EPS grows at 8–10 per cent annually. The P/E remains in the 45–55× range, delivering high-single-digit to low-double-digit annual total returns. A perfectly respectable outcome for a best-in-class retailer.
      Weight: 50%
    
    
      Bear Case
      50 / 100
      A consumer spending slowdown disproportionately hits warehouse retailers as middle-income members trade down to discount formats. Sam's Club and BJ's increase competitive pressure. Comp sales turn negative for several quarters. The P/E contracts to 35–40× as growth expectations reset lower. The stock falls 20–25 per cent in a risk-off environment.
      Weight: 20%

recommendation.text:
Conviction Score: 68/100 — HOLD
  The shares trade 6.4 per cent below their 52-week high at 51.9× earnings — a premium that is justified by the quality of the franchise but leaves very limited near-term upside. Costco is one of the world's finest retailers, but at this valuation, long-term investors are better off building positions gradually on dips rather than initiating at current levels.
  
    Buy Below$870
    HOLD Zone$870–$1,067
    Reduce Above$1,067
    Stop / Sell$800

entryExit.text:
COST — Costco Wholesale Corp (COST) — Entry / Exit Framework

Reduce / Trim: Above $1018.44 — the stock is trading near or above the 52-week high. Capital should be rotated elsewhere.
Tighten or exit on any bounce above $1098.32 — the risk/reward is poor at current levels.
Stop-loss (for remaining exposure): $1088.42 — a close above the 52-week high would confirm the bull case is breaking down.
Target: $911.58 — partial mean reversion toward the middle of the 52-week range.
